Covers layout patterns, theme system, visual asset inventory/cleanup, image gap analysis, auth cookie debugging, and deployment follow-through." version: 1.4.0 tags: [nextjs, ecommerce, admin, audit, tailwind, layout, sidebar, supabase, auth, cloudflare, org-restructuring, package-extraction] --- # Ecommerce Codebase Audit Inspect and improve a Next.js e-commerce admin panel. Focus on layout consistency, theme application, page structure, image assets, and auth flow. ## Audit Checklist ### Layout Consistency - [ ] Every admin page wraps content in `AdminShell` from `@/components/admin/admin-layout` - [ ] `AdminShell` includes the sidebar navigation (responsive: hidden on mobile, fixed on desktop) - [ ] Sidebar uses the same links and active-state highlighting across all pages - [ ] All pages render inside `` not just individually ### Theme System The admin theme uses emerald/zinc on a dark background (#0a0a0b / #0f0f10): ```tsx // Tailwind classes to look for bg-[#0f0f10] // sidebar + card backgrounds bg-zinc-800/60 // borders text-emerald-400 // active text bg-emerald-500/10 // active background text-zinc-400 // inactive text hover:bg-zinc-800/40 // hover state ``` - [ ] All pages use the same color tokens (not hardcoded different colors) - [ ] Active page shows `bg-emerald-500/10 text-emerald-400` with indicator dot - [ ] Inactive pages use `text-zinc-400 hover:text-zinc-200 hover:bg-zinc-800/40` ### Page Structure Each admin page follows this pattern: ```tsx 'use client' import { AdminShell, useAdminAuth } from "@/components/admin/admin-layout" import { useState, useEffect } from "react" export default function PageName() { const { authed } = useAdminAuth() if (!authed) return null return ( <>

Page Title

{/* content */} ) } ``` - [ ] Pages use `{ authed } = useAdminAuth()` for auth guard - [ ] Pages use `AdminShell` wrapper (already in the root layout) - [ ] Pages use `fetch("/api/admin/*")` for data (not direct Supabase client) - [ ] Data fetched via `/api/admin/` routes that use service_role key server-side ### AdminShell Component Located in `@/components/admin/admin-layout` (or wherever it's imported from). The AdminShell provides: - Sidebar navigation with 4 groups: Operaciones, Catálogo, Marketing, Configuración - Mobile responsiveness (hidden sidebar on mobile, toggle with hamburger) - Auth guard via `useAdminAuth` - Consistent background and layout padding ## Auth Flow & Cookie Debugging ### The Core Trap: Two Auth Paths Crossing In Next.js + Supabase e-commerce projects, there are usually TWO auth paths that can conflict: **Path A — Consumer auth (useAuth):** - Login page calls `supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()` directly from the browser client - Supabase's `@supabase/ssr` browser client sets the session cookie automatically - `/mi-cuenta` reads session via `supabase.auth.getSession()` — works because cookie exists **Path B — Admin auth (useAdminAuth / /api/auth):** - Login page calls `/api/auth` with `action: "login"` - API route creates an SSR client with `createServerClient()`, but often has `setAll() {}` empty - **The session cookie never gets set on the response** — the API returns tokens, but no cookie - Admin layout falls back to localStorage or custom cookie **Common failure modes:** 1. **Login works (gets tokens) but /mi-cuenta shows unauthenticated** — consumer login page fetches `/api/auth` instead of calling Supabase directly. Fix: use `useAuth().login()` (Supabase browser client) which sets the correct session cookie. 2. **Login works but /admin redirects back to /login** — admin layout's `useAdminAuth()` calls `/api/auth?action=me` with a token from localStorage, but the API route's token verification path fails. Fix: ensure the API route can verify tokens against Supabase REST API (not just SSR cookies). ### Fix Checklist - [ ] Consumer login uses Supabase browser client directly (`supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()`), NOT a custom `/api/auth` endpoint - [ ] After successful login, store session tokens in `localStorage` for admin layout fallback: `localStorage.setItem("elviajero_admin_session", JSON.stringify({ access_token, refresh_token }))` - [ ] Admin layout checks localStorage first, then Supabase SSR cookie, then custom cookie — triple fallback - [ ] API route `action: "me"` verifies tokens via `fetch(${supabaseUrl}/auth/v1/user)` not `supabase.auth.getUser()` - [ ] Service-role client for profile queries uses `createClient(url, serviceRoleKey)` — passing service key as second arg, not in headers - [ ] Role check queries DB profiles table (not JWT claim) — `profiles.role` not `user.role` ### Debugging Auth Issues ```bash # Check if API route is reachable curl -sX POST https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/api/auth \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"action":"login","email":"[REDACTED_EMAIL]","password":"test"}' | head -c 200 # Check if session cookie is set curl -sv https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/mi-cuenta 2>&1 | grep -i 'set-cookie' # Check server logs docker service logs elviajero-comercio_web --tail 30 --no-task-ids ``` ## Visual Asset Workflow ### Full Asset Overhaul Pattern When doing a complete visual assets redesign (inventory, generation, deployment): #### Phase 1: Inventory ```bash # List all files on disk find public/images -type f | sort # List all code references grep -rohP '"/images/[^"'"'"']+' --include='*.{tsx,ts,json,css}' | sed 's/^"//' | sort -u # Cross-reference: 3 outcomes per image # 1. Referenced + on disk = OK # 2. Referenced + missing = FIX (add file) # 3. On disk + no reference = ORPHAN (delete) # Size audit — small = programmatic (low quality) find public/images -name "*.png" -size -50k ``` #### Phase 2: Categorization | Type | Size threshold | Keep as | Upgrade? | |------|---------------|---------|----------| | SVGs | varies | Vector | Only if they're emoji text | | Real photos | >50KB | PNG | No | | Programmatic renders | <50KB | PNG | Yes — regenerate with richer Pillow composites | | Icons | 4-8KB | PNG | Intentionally small — minimalist silhouettes | | Hero backgrounds | <20KB | PNG | Yes — add atmosphere, silhouettes, gradients | #### Phase 3: Generation (no AI API available) Use Pillow for progressive enhancement rounds: **Round 1 — Placeholders:** ```python # Sky gradient + bokeh circles + category silhouettes # Write to a generate script, deploy, iterate ``` **Round 2 — Richer details:** - Tree silhouettes, stars, glowing tent, moon - River reflections, fishing rod, sun at horizon - Mountain trails, hiker with backpack - Headlight beams, road lines, car approaching - Dust clouds, motorcycle, fence - Fields, barn, sun, clouds **Round 3 — AI (if REPLICATE_API_TOKEN or equivalent available):** ```python if call_replicate(prompt, w, h, path): print("AI generated") else: render_fallback(path) # keep best Pillow version ``` **Round 4 — Self-critique:** After deploying, review each asset and write a brutal critique doc. Then regenerate the worst offenders in the next round. #### Phase 4: Deployment Pitfalls **Cloudflare cache (IMPORTANT):** - CF sets `cache-control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable` — 1 YEAR cache - No CF API key? Only option is **rename the file** (logo.png → logo-v2.png) - Then update ALL code references to point to new filename - Verify with curl, not browser: `curl -sk URL/images/logo-v2.png -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %{size_download}B"` - Then tell the user to hard refresh (Cmd+Shift+R) **Docker layer cache:** - If files exist locally but return 404 on deploy, the Docker COPY was cached - Fix: `docker build --no-cache` or touch a file in the COPY layer **Verify the right file is served:** ```bash # Check HTML references curl -sk https://example.com/ | grep -oP '(logo|favicon)-v2' # Check file directly curl -sk https://example.com/images/logo-v2.png -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %{size_download}B" ``` ### Key Project-Specific Patterns (El Viajero) - Product images: `content/es.json` → `/images/marketing/products/*.png` (34 products) - Category backgrounds (dynamic SVG): `/images/categories/${slug}.svg` — slug values in `app/categoria/*/page.tsx` - Category heroes: heroImage prop → `app/categoria/*/page.tsx` → `/images/marketing/hero-*.png` - Hero carousel: content-driven array in `content/es.json` → `/images/marketing/hero-carousel-*.png` - Testimonials: `"image"` field in `content/es.json` → `/images/testimonials/avatar-*.png` - Logo: `header.tsx` → `/images/logo.png` (or `logo-v2.png` after cache-bust) - Favicon: `layout.tsx` → `/images/favicon.png` (or `favicon-v2.png`) - OG image: `layout.tsx` → `/images/og-viajero.svg` - PWA icons: `manifest.json` → `/images/icon-192.png`, `/images/icon-512.png` ### Generation Recipes **Category icons (128x128):** Create a Python script with `ImageDraw` — tent, fish, umbrella, backpack, car, motorcycle, barn silhouettes on green circle background. These are intentionally small (4-8KB). **Hero banners (1920x600):** Create scene composites: 1. Sky gradient (top to bottom, warm/cool depending on scene) 2. Ground layer (dark rectangle) 3. Scene elements (tree silhouettes, stars, moon, tent, river, fishing rod, mountains, road, headlights, barn, fence, sun) 4. Bokeh overlay (random circles with low alpha) 5. Gaussian blur (radius 0.3-0.5) for polish **Testimonial avatars (200x200):** If no AI available, create colored circles with basic face features (eyes, smile, hair shape, shirt). Tell the client these are placeholders for real customer photos. **PWA icons:** Render from the logo at 192x192 and 512x512 with anti-aliasing. ## Multi-Locale Content Updates (PAB Sites) When updating content across all locales (en/es/nl/de) in a PAB (Paragu-AI Builder) project: ### Safe approach vs. pitfalls 1. **Python JSON manipulation is safer than `patch` tool for large content changes.** The `patch` tool works well for small string replacements, but when replacing JSON blocks spanning 50+ lines, it can match the wrong text, leave orphaned content, and produce valid-but-wrong JSON. This happened when replacing the `processPage` section — patch matched a subset, leaving trailing orphan that was syntactically valid but structurally wrong. 2. **When `patch` goes wrong: stop, don't stack.** If the first patch produces unexpected results (orphan content, duplicated sections, lost keys), do NOT apply more patches on top. Switch immediately to Python `json.load()/json.dump()` — read the broken file, fix the data structure in memory, and write it back clean. Stacking patches compounds the mistakes and makes the file increasingly fragile to further edits. The `processPage` fix required three patches to clean up what one wrong match did. 3. **`patch` can match content from a previously-applied patch** — If a file was already modified, the old_string might not match what's actually in the file. Always read the file fresh before patching, and verify with a direct key inspection after (`python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('f')); print(d.get('targetKey',{}).keys())"`). 4. **Shell variable expansion fails in `execute_code`'s multi-line Python strings** — `for f in $FILES; do python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('$f'))"; done` breaks because `$f` isn't expanded inside the Python string. Use explicit file paths, a temp script, or Python's own `glob` module instead. 5. **Always validate JSON structure after content changes, not just syntax.** A file can be valid JSON but structurally wrong (missing keys, wrong step counts, orphan content). After validation: ```bash python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('file'))" # syntax check ``` Also verify structural integrity: ```python d["processPage"]["process"]["steps"] # should have 5 steps d["home"]["services"]["groups"] # should have 3 groups d["aboutPage"]["story"]["paragraphs"] # should have 3 paragraphs ``` 6. **When deleting orphaned content from a botched patch, read the file with JSON parser first**, then find the structural key that contains the orphan data. Delete it from the Python dict and re-write. Don't try to surgically remove lines with patch. 7. **Process step consistency rule:** The same process narrative lives in 6 places across the site. Update ALL of these together in the same script block — missing any one creates inconsistency: - `home.process.steps` (homepage) - `processPage.process.steps` (process page) - `landingInversor.process.steps`, `landingTrust.process.steps`, `landingLifestyle.process.steps`, `landingEmpresa.process.steps` (LinkedIn ad landing pages) 8. **Process for adding new content sections across locales:** - Step 1: Define the content data structure in a Python dict with all localizations - Step 2: Load each locale file, apply transformations, write back - Step 3: Validate JSON syntax for all files - Step 4: Verify structural keys match expectations (count groups, steps, paragraphs) ```python for lang in ["en", "es", "nl", "de"]: with open(f"content/{lang}.json") as f: d = json.load(f) d["parentSection"]["newKey"] = translations[lang] with open(f"content/{lang}.json", "w") as f: json.dump(d, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False) ``` 9. **Section structure for the 'Our Services' pattern:** ```python data["home"]["services"] = { "eyebrow": "Our Services", "title": "Section title", "groups": [ {"id": "group-id", "title": "Group Name", "subtitle": "Group subtitle.", "items": [{"title": "Service", "description": "Desc.", "icon": "IconName"}]} ] } ``` 10. **Section structure for the 'Story' pattern:** ```python data["aboutPage"]["story"] = { "title": "Our Story", "eyebrow": "Tagline", "paragraphs": ["Para 1.", "Para 2.", "Para 3."] } ``` 11. **Section structure for the 'Process steps' pattern:** ```python data["processPage"]["process"]["steps"] = [ {"number": 1, "title": "Step Name", "description": "Long description.", "icon": "IconName", "image": {"$img": "process.key"}, "duration": "Week 1"} ] ``` 12. **Landing page Calendly URL pattern:** ```python base = "https://calendly.com/nexaparaguay/consulta" utm = f"utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=paid&utm_campaign={campaign}" url = f"{base}?{utm}&utm_content=cta-{placement}-{lang}" ``` ## Deployment ### Docker Deployment After modifying content assets (images, content JSON, or static files): 1. **Rebuild** — `npm run build` catches missing images and type errors 2. **Rebuild Docker image** — `docker build -t elviajero-comercio:prod .` 3. **Deploy** — `docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml elviajero-comercio --detach=false` 4. **Verify** — `curl -sI https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/ | head -5` 5. **Force rolling update** — if same image tag, `docker service update --force elviajero-comercio_web` Never assume changes to static content or images are live — Docker rebuild and stack deploy are required. Browser cache is NOT the server — always curl first. ### Production Monitoring After every deploy, add: - Healthcheck endpoint at `/api/health` returning `{status, timestamp, uptime}` - Docker HEALTHCHECK in Dockerfile (every 30s, 40s start grace, 3 retries) - Cron healthcheck via Hermes: every 15m, auto-redeploy on failure - Shell check script: `/root/scripts/check-{project}.sh` ### Docker Build Timeout `docker build` with npm install can take >120s on cold cache. Set terminal timeout to 300s for builds. If it times out, retry — the npm cache is warm on the second run and it will complete faster. #### Next.js `pages/` Folder Conflict (v16+) Next.js 16+ errors with "`pages` and `app` directories should be under the same folder" when a `pages/` data directory exists at project root alongside `src/app/`. Fix: 1. Rename `pages/` to `nexa-pages/` 2. Update all `page.tsx` references from `'pages'` to `'nexa-pages'` 3. Update Dockerfile to copy `nexa-pages/` into the standalone output 4. Run `docker build --no-cache` after rename (Docker layer cache may use old path) #### Docker Standalone + Data Files Next.js `output: "standalone"` produces a `server.js` that does `process.chdir(__dirname)` — so `process.cwd()` points to `.next/standalone/`, not the app root. Data files read at runtime via `readFileSync(path.join(process.cwd(), ...))` must be copied into `.next/standalone/`: ```dockerfile COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./ COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/nexa-pages ./.next/standalone/nexa-pages COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./.next/standalone/content ``` Without this, the container runs fine but returns static HTML with fallback data because `readFileSync` silently fails and the catch block returns defaults. #### The "Welcome" Bug (Static SSG Fallback) When a PAB page always renders "Welcome" regardless of content changes, the root cause is in the page component's fallback chain: ```tsx // BROKEN: home.json has 'titleKey' not 'title' — always falls through {homePage.title || content?.title || 'Welcome'} // FIXED: use siteName from the locale JSON {content?.siteName || 'Nexa Paraguay'} ``` Check that `getPages()` and `getContent()` actually read files via `readFileSync`/`readdirSync` at build time. The `catch` block silently swallowing the error is the symptom — verify the files are accessible during the Docker build stage. ## Communication Style (Ivan/ParaguAI) This user gives short commands and wants short responses. Key rules: - **No walls of text.** Deliver analysis in tables/bullets, not paragraphs. - **No explanations of what you're about to do.** Just do it and report what changed. - **Batch operations always.** Never one file at a time for repetitive work. - **One recommendation, not options.** Do not list alternatives unless asked. - **Final output: what changed, then stop.** No "Would you like me to...?" follow-ups. - **Deploy automatically.** If you changed something, build + deploy + verify without being asked. - **Hard refresh solves browser cache.** When user says "I still see old images", confirm server serves new file (curl), then tell them to hard refresh. Do NOT re-deploy. - **Phrasing: "Done." + summary + nothing else.** Test: if your response has more than 5 items of information after "Done.", it's too long. ## Full-Site Audit Methodology (PAB Static Sites) When performing a full site content audit on a PAB multi-locale site like nexa-paraguay, follow this 20-point hierarchy. Each finding gets: status emoji (✅/🟡/🔴), priority label, and actionable next step. [...existing 20 points...] ## Post-Audit: Repo Enhancement Research (Optional) After the audit identifies gaps, consider running `repo-enhancement-research` on the target repo to discover: - Alternative frameworks/libraries that do things better - High-star templates with patterns to steal (admin dashboards, auth flows, checkout) - Dependency upgrades (better-sqlite3 → supabase, framer-motion → CSS animations, etc.) ```bash python3 /root/.hermes/skills/software-development/repo-enhancement-research/scripts/search.py /path/to/repo ``` This is most valuable when the audit reveals: stale dependencies, missing features competitors have, or admin panel gaps. It is NOT needed for purely content/skin changes. ## Cross-Repo Standard & Base Package Architecture When working across multiple client repos in the same org (Ai-Whisperers), the client repos share no code. The most advanced repo (typically elviajero-comercio) has the richest feature set. The best practice is: ### Step 1: Feature inventory Compare each repo's feature set against elviajero's reference standard: - 104+ components vs <10 - 18 admin pages vs 0 - auth (Supabase SSR + OAuth) vs nothing - 4 payment gateways vs 0 - cart/checkout vs nothing - 3 locales vs 0 - shadcn/ui vs bare tailwind - Docker multi-stage + healthcheck vs basic - PWA + manifest vs nothing - SEO (JSON-LD, sitemap, RSS) vs nothing ### Step 2: Extract shared packages Create a base repo (`@ai-whisperers/base`) that publishes npm packages: ```bash packages/ ui/ → @ai-whisperers/ui (Button, Card, Dialog, DataTable, Toast, etc.) commerce/ → @ai-whisperers/commerce (cart, checkout, payment gateways, products) admin/ → @ai-whisperers/admin (AdminLayout, StatCard, charts, ImageUpload) auth/ → @ai-whisperers/auth (AuthProvider, OAuth, middleware, Supabase SSR) whatsapp/ → @ai-whisperers/whatsapp (WhatsAppFloat, share, templates) seo/ → @ai-whisperers/seo (JSON-LD, OG, Analytics, CookieConsent) i18n/ → @ai-whisperers/i18n (locales, language switcher, currency) theme/ → @ai-whisperers/theme (Tailwind preset, tokens, fonts) ``` ### Step 3: Client repo migration Each client repo installs `@aiw/*` packages and replaces local copies: ```json { "dependencies": { "@ai-whisperers/ui": "^1.0.0", "@ai-whisperers/auth": "^1.0.0", "@ai-whisperers/commerce": "^1.0.0" } } ``` ### Step 4: Client-specific code stays Each client keeps: content JSON, images, page layouts, business-logic pages (about, contact, blog, FAQ, terms), Dockerfile, docker-compose, config files. ### Tool Usage Pitfall: read_file + write_file Corrupts Content **CRITICAL BUG:** The `read_file` tool returns content with `LINE_NUM|` prefixes (e.g., `1|"use client"\n2|import ...`). If you pass this content directly to `write_file`, the line numbers are written into the file, corrupting it. **Symptoms:** - TS errors: `TS1109: Expression expected` at every line - Node.js `require()` fails with unexpected tokens - `head -5` shows `1| "use client"` (the `1|` is literal) **Prevention:** 1. For simple reads, use `terminal(f"cat {path}")` instead of `read_file` when the content will be re-written 2. OR strip the prefix before writing: ```python import re content = re.sub(r'^\s*\d+\|', '', content, flags=re.MULTILINE) ``` 3. For bulk operations, use a cleanup script after any batch write: ```python def clean_file(path): with open(path) as f: content = f.read() cleaned = re.sub(r'^\s*\d+\|', '', content, flags=re.MULTILINE) if cleaned != content: with open(path, 'w') as f: f.write(cleaned) return True return False ``` ### Client Dependency Strategy: file: -> github: -> npm When client installs `@ai-whisperers/*` packages, dependency strategy depends on context: | Context | Strategy | Path Pattern | |---------|----------|-------------| | Local dev | `file:` (relative) | `"file:../base/packages/ui"` | | CI/CD build | `github:` tag | `"github:Ai-Whisperers/base#v0.1.0"` | | External | npm (GitHub Packages) | `"^0.1.0"` with `.npmrc` | **Secret scanning trap:** `file:/root/...` paths trigger GitHub push protection ("Push cannot contain secrets"). Use relative `file:../` or `github:` URLs before pushing to repos with branch protection. ### Package API Naming Mismatches When elviajero's internal functions differ from the package exports: | elviajero (old) | @ai-whisperers/* (new) | Why | |-----------------|----------------------|-----| | `getAdminClient()` | `createAdminClient()` | Packages use factory pattern | | `CartItem.imageUrl` | `CartItem.image` | Cleaner field name | | `Order.created_at` | `Order.createdAt` | camelCase convention | | `middleware.ts: updateSession()` | Inline in middleware | Avoid Next.js instance collision | Fix: global sed for batch renames, then individual patches for type mismatches. ### Type Compatibility: snake_case <-> camelCase Supabase returns snake_case (`created_at`, `customer_name`). Package types use camelCase (`createdAt`, `customerName`). **Best fix:** Add both shapes and an index signature: ```typescript export interface Order { id: string createdAt?: string // camelCase (package convention) customerName?: string created_at?: string // snake_case (Supabase) customer_name?: string [key: string]: any // allow any field } ``` ### next/headers cookies() Async (Next.js 15+) In Next.js 15, `cookies()` and `headers()` return Promises: ```typescript // OLD: const cookieStore = cookies() cookieStore.getAll() // NEW (Next.js 15): const cookieStore = await cookies() cookieStore.getAll() ``` Server-side Supabase client callbacks must be async: ```typescript cookies: { async getAll() { const store = await cookieStore; return store.getAll() }, async setAll(cookiesToSet) { const store = await cookieStore; /* ... */ } } ``` ### Package Extraction: Rewriting @/ Imports When extracting components from a Next.js app into standalone npm packages, three import patterns need fixing: 1. **`@/content/es.json`** — This is an app-specific content file. Replace with prop-based config or remove entirely. The package should NOT depend on this file. ```tsx // BEFORE: import content from "@/content/es.json" // AFTER: the component accepts a config prop or provides defaults ``` 2. **`@/lib/*`** — App-specific utility imports. Either: - Rewrite to relative imports if the utility is extracted into the same package - Replace with a stub/comment if the utility should come from the consumer app - Import from another `@ai-whisperers/*` package if the utility lives there 3. **`../../lib/cart-context`** style paths — Files extracted into subdirectories (e.g., `cart/`) may have relative paths that point outside the package. Fix by: - Creating the target files within the package (e.g., `src/types.ts`, `src/cart-context.tsx`) - Correcting the relative paths to point within `src/` **Verification:** After fixing imports, run `npx tsc --noEmit` in the package to catch all remaining issues in one pass, then fix them all before rebuilding. ### Step 5: CI/CD workflow migration After package extraction and npm publish, migrate client repos from standalone deploy.yml to reusable ci-cd workflows: ```yaml # .github/workflows/ci.yml name: CI on: pull_request: branches: [main] push: branches: [main] jobs: ci: uses: Ai-Whisperers/ci-cd/.github/workflows/ci-nextjs.yml@main with: supabase-url: '[REDACTED_SUPABASE_URL]' supabase-anon-key: 'placeholder' # .github/workflows/deploy.yml name: Deploy on: push: branches: [main] jobs: deploy: uses: Ai-Whisperers/ci-cd/.github/workflows/deploy-vps.yml@main with: site-name: ${{ github.event.repository.name }} secrets: VPS_HOST: ${{ secrets.VPS_HOST }} VPS_USER: ${{ secrets.VPS_USER }} VPS_KEY: ${{ secrets.VPS_KEY }} ``` Push via GitHub API: ```python import base64 ci_b64 = base64.b64encode(ci_yml.encode()).decode() subprocess.run(["gh", "api", f"repos/Ai-Whisperers/{repo}/contents/.github/workflows/ci.yml", "--method", "PUT", "-f", "message=chore: add CI workflow", "-f", f"content={ci_b64}"]) ``` The VPS needs a deploy script at `/opt/stacks/ci-cd/scripts/deploy.sh`: ```bash #!/bin/bash set -e SITE_NAME="$1"; REPO="$2"; SITE_DIR="/root/$SITE_NAME" if [ -d "$SITE_DIR" ]; then cd "$SITE_DIR"; git fetch origin; git reset --hard origin/main else git clone "https://github.com/$REPO.git" "$SITE_DIR"; cd "$SITE_DIR"; fi npm ci 2>/dev/null || npm install; npm run build if [ -f "docker-compose.yml" ]; then docker compose pull 2>/dev/null || true; docker compose up -d --build elif [ -f "Dockerfile" ]; then docker build -t "$SITE_NAME:latest" .; docker compose up -d --build; fi ``` ### Step 6: Monitoring stack deployment Deploy Prometheus + Grafana on the VPS for container-level observability: ```yaml # /opt/monitoring/docker-compose.yml services: prometheus: image: prom/prometheus:latest ports: ["9090:9090"] volumes: ["./prometheus:/etc/prometheus", "prometheus_data:/prometheus"] node-exporter: image: prom/node-exporter:latest pid: host volumes: ["/:/host:ro,rslave"] cadvisor: image: gcr.io/cadvisor/cadvisor:latest volumes: ["/:/rootfs:ro", "/var/run:/var/run:ro", "/sys:/sys:ro", "/var/lib/docker/:/var/lib/docker:ro"] privileged: true grafana: image: grafana/grafana:latest ports: ["3001:3000"] environment: - GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_PASSWORD: [REDACTED] - GF_AUTH_ANONYMOUS_ENABLED=true volumes: ["grafana_data:/var/lib/grafana"] ``` Prometheus config (`/opt/monitoring/prometheus/prometheus.yml`): ```yaml global: scrape_interval: 15s scrape_configs: - job_name: "node" static_configs: [targets: ["node-exporter:9100"]] - job_name: "cadvisor" static_configs: [targets: ["cadvisor:8080"]] ``` Pitfalls: - Monitoring containers use their own `monitoring` Docker network — they can't reach Traefik's network unless connected - Cadvisor needs `privileged: true` and `pid: host` for node-exporter - Fresh containers show "unknown" health for first scrape cycle (~15s), then go to "up" - Grafana default creds: admin/admin — disable anonymous auth in production - **Grafana Traefik routing**: Deploy grafana as a SWARM service (not standalone compose) on the same network as Traefik (`agent-net`). Attach traefik labels directly. Standalone compose containers can't share networks with Swarm services. - **lucide-react in deps**: Any package importing lucide-react must have it in `dependencies`, not just `peerDependencies`. The CI publish workflow runs `npm ci` which skips peerDeps. ### Step 7: Template update Update `template-nextjs-client` to pre-install all `@aiw/*` packages and include ci.yml + deploy.yml so new client sites start with the full feature set. ## Full-Site Audit Methodology (PAB Static Sites)** — nav-linked vs orphan pages 2. **SEO** — titles, descriptions, schema markup (LocalBusiness, Service, FAQ, Article) 3. **Content completeness** — locale comparison, missing keys, translation quality flags 4. **Services** — structure, dedicated page, nav links, consistency with questionnaire 5. **Story/About** — founder narrative, team info, personal touch 6. **Process steps** — consistency across all pages that display process (home, process page, landing pages) 7. **Landing pages** — LinkedIn ad pages, Calendly URL consistency, UTM params 8. **Pricing** — packages vs a-la-carte, questionnaire alignment 9. **Testimonials** — real client names vs AI-generated, consent status 10. **Contact info** — phone, WhatsApp, email, address, hours, Calendly, booking URL 11. **Footer** — columns, links, social profiles, missing links 12. **Placeholders & TODOs** — categorize by severity (🔴 blocks launch, 🟡 quality, 🟢 nice-to-have) 13. **Analytics & integrations** — GA4, HubSpot, Mailchimp real vs placeholder IDs 14. **Deployment** — Docker, Traefik, CDN, domain, staging 15. **Image assets** — webp/png coverage, placeholder status, alt text quality 16. **Brand tokens** — colors, typography, logo variants 17. **Compliance** — GDPR, AML, SEPRELAD, privacy policy, license numbers 18. **Conversion flow** — user journey from landing to booking, post-booking follow-up 19. **Content questionnaire alignment** — cross-reference every phrase from the client's response against current site 20. **Content questionnaire cross-reference** — after making content changes based on client responses, run a final phrase-level check: extract every substantive claim/description from the client's written response and verify it exists verbatim (or semantically equivalent) in the current site content. | Topic | Lesson |

| Changesets | Don't use `changesets/action@v1` wrapper (org blocks PR creation). Use `npx changeset publish` directly |
| Inter-package deps | Use `file:../auth` NOT `workspace:*` (npm workspaces protocol fails against GitHub Packages) |
| Peer deps | Must also be in `dependencies` (tsc resolves them at build time) |
| Stale changesets | `cat .changeset/*.md` — remove any referencing packages that no longer exist |
| Client setup | Each consumer repo needs its own `.npmrc` + `NODE_AUTH_TOKEN` in deploy env |
